content.full_text:
November 17, 1977

Mr. J. L. Evans
United Transportation Union
921 11th Street, Suite 502
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Evans:

Thank you for your letter dated October 26, 1977, requesting in-
formation regarding the lobbying provisions of the Political
Reform Act.

First, you have asked if the definition of "administrative action"
has been amended since June of 1976. Neither the definition of
"administrative action" contained in Section 82602 of the Political
Reform Act nor the definition of "quasi-legislative administrative
action" contained in Section 18202 of Title 2 of the California
Administrative Code has been amended. Therefore, the discussion
of "administrative action" on page three of the "Instruction Manual
for Form 630", published in June 1976, reflects the current law
and regulations.

Second, you have inquired if a registered lobbyist or an employer
of a lobbyist must report expenditures related to influencing
agency actions which are not "quasi-legislative administrative
actions", by virtue of Section 18202 of Title 2 of the California
Administrative Code. There is nothing in the Political Reform
Act or the Commission's regulations that requires a lobbyist or a
lobbyist employer to report expenditures in connection with in-
fluencing agency action that is not an administrative action.
However, it should be noted, that every payment which benefits an
official of a state agency which is or should be listed on the
lobbyist's registration statement must be disclosed regardless of
whether the specific action the lobbyist or the employer is at-
tempting to influence at that particular time is an administrative
action.

Third, you have inquired about the reporting requirements of a
lobbyist who testifies before a state agency on behalf of his or
her employer. Testimony before a state agency by a lobbyist on
behalf of his or her employer is influencing administrative action,
so long as the testimony is related to a rate-making proceeding or
a quasi-legislative proceeding. Accordingly, a lobbyist should

Page Two
November 17, 1977
Mr. J. L. Evans

disclose on his or her periodic report, the specific administrative
action he or she attempted to prevent, the influence by providing public testimony
and all of the expenses associated with that testimony.

If I can be of further assistance, do not hesitate to contact me.

Very truly yours,


Alan Herndon
Compliance Representative


AH:vh

TO: [x]JEANNE          [ ] JAY                          FROM:  Carla

    [ ]LYNN             [ ] BARBARA

    [ ]RICH             [ ] BOB S.

    [ ]HELEN            [x] OTHER Mary Ann


[x]TELEPHONE ADVICE - SEE BELOW

[ ]CORRESPONDENCE ADVICE - SEE ATTACHED DRAFT
     (IF YOU HAVE ANY COMMENTS, NOTIFY ADVISOR WITHIN 1 DAY)

FILE LOCATION:  82002, 18202

INCLUDE IN ADVICE PACKAGE:          YES [ ]          NO [x]


CALLER     Gail

REPRESENTING Cohen, Stokke & Davis

PHONE NUMBER: 442-3130


QUESTION: Company A and Company B are both prospective bidders on a city
          contract.  Company A has requested a state agency to exempt them
          from certain state requirements so that they can lower costs.
          Company B has hired the law/lobbying firm CS&D to represent them
          before the state agency in opposing Company A's request.  Is
          it reportable on CS&D's lobbying reports?

ANSWER:

     No.  The activities are exempt under regulation 18202(a)(3)
     which states that a proceeding to enforce compliance with
     existing law is not a quasi-legislative proceeding.

No. The activities are exempt under regulation 18202(a)(3) which states that a proceeding to enforce compliance with existing law is not a quasi-legislative proceeding.

First, you have asked if the definition of "administrative action" has been amended since June of 1976.
